text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY CLINIC NOTE
Attending Physician: [Attending Oncologist]
Clinical Service: Genitourinary Oncology

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 71-year-old Polish-American male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) who presents today for follow-up after restaging imaging demonstrating disease progression through two cycles of carboplatin and cabazitaxel. He is accompanied by his wife and adult daughter.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Kowalski is a 71-year-old retired steelworker with a 45 pack-year smoking history (quit at age 62) and significant comorbidities including type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and chronic kidney disease (baseline eGFR ~42-52 mL/min at diagnosis, now declining). He was initially diagnosed at age 67 with metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer (mHSPC), Gleason 9 (5+4) Grade Group 5, with M1b disease involving the axial and appendicular skeleton (8 discrete bone lesions at diagnosis) and bilateral pelvic lymph node involvement. PSA at diagnosis was 187 ng/mL. Germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant (p.E1308*) was identified at diagnosis.

He was treated with ADT (leuprolide acetate) plus abiraterone/prednisone as initial therapy, with an excellent PSA response (nadir 1.1 ng/mL). He developed CRPC at approximately age 69 years with rising PSA and new bone lesions, at which point abiraterone was discontinued and enzalutamide was initiated. He had a second PSA response on enzalutamide (nadir 1.4 ng/mL) but again developed resistance at approximately age 70.2 years, with worsening osseous disease and pelvic/retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. Palliative radiation was delivered to the right femoral neck for pain control at age 70.3 years. Olaparib was then initiated given his BRCA2 germline mutation (per PROfound trial), with initial PSA response (PSA nadir approximately 4.8 ng/mL). However, his course on olaparib was complicated by severe, recurrent anemia requiring multiple packed red blood cell transfusions and ultimately two dose reductions (from 300 mg BID to 200 mg BID and then to 100 mg BID). Repeat restaging imaging at age 71 years revealed new hepatic lesions (two lesions in segments VI-VII of the right lobe), which were confirmed by gadolinium-enhanced MRI and PET/CT to represent hepatic metastases. CT-guided liver biopsy confirmed metastatic adenocarcinoma of prostatic origin with focal synaptophysin positivity and a markedly elevated Ki-67 of approximately 60%, raising concern for early treatment-emergent neuroendocrine prostate cancer (t-NEPC). Repeat NGS on the liver biopsy demonstrated retention of BRCA2 p.E1308* without reversion mutations, but notably acquired AR amplification, AR p.L702H ligand-binding domain mutation, PIK3CA p.E545K, and AURKA amplification -- consistent with a complex, multi-resistant phenotype.

Given the neuroendocrine features, BRCA2 mutation without reversion, and platinum sensitivity rationale, the decision was made at multidisciplinary tumor board to transition to carboplatin (AUC 4) plus cabazitaxel (20 mg/m2) every 21 days with prophylactic G-CSF. Olaparib was discontinued. ADT (leuprolide) was continued.

Cycle 1 was complicated by febrile neutropenia with ANC nadir of 0.3 K/uL, requiring 5-day hospitalization, IV piperacillin-tazobactam, and 2 units of packed RBCs for grade 3 anemia (Hgb 6.3 g/dL). A transient AKI (creatinine peak 2.1 mg/dL) occurred during that hospitalization. Cabazitaxel was dose-reduced to 15 mg/m2 and carboplatin to AUC 3 for subsequent cycles.

Restaging CT after 2 cycles of carboplatin/cabazitaxel (completed approximately 2-3 weeks prior to today's visit) showed clear radiographic progression: the dominant hepatic lesions in segment VI-VII have grown from 1.2 cm and 1.0 cm to 1.4 cm and 1.3 cm, with a new 0.6 cm lesion in segment V. The previously indeterminate right lower lobe pulmonary nodules have increased in size (9 mm from 5 mm, and 6 mm from 4 mm), now meeting criteria for concern for pulmonary metastases. The right obturator lymph node has progressed to 2.4 cm. PSA at time of imaging was 18.6 ng/mL.

He presents today to discuss the implications of these findings, explore further treatment options, and to meet with palliative care.

Since the last visit, the patient describes worsening overall functional status. He is spending the majority of his waking hours in bed or in his recliner chair and requires significant assistance for activities of daily living. He reports severe fatigue that is limiting all activity. He continues to have diffuse bone pain, worst in the lumbar spine, pelvis, and right hip (treatment field), which he rates at approximately 5/10 on average with his current analgesic regimen. He has not had any acute fractures. He reports significant anorexia with markedly reduced oral intake over the past 4-6 weeks -- he estimates eating roughly 25-30% of his usual meals. He has lost approximately 15 lbs total over the past 5 months. He endorses moderate dyspnea with minimal exertion (walking to the bathroom), which is a new symptom over the past few weeks. No chest pain, hemoptysis, or pleuritic pain. He reports intermittent nausea (grade 1) at home, manageable with oral ondansetron. He denies vomiting. He has had mild lower extremity swelling, left greater than right. He denies new neurological symptoms: no focal weakness, no bowel or bladder incontinence, no saddle anesthesia. He endorses feeling that his illness is progressing and has had extensive conversations with his family. He and his wife have been preparing for what lies ahead.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Significant weight loss (15 lbs over 5 months), severe fatigue, decreased appetite, generalized malaise. No fevers or chills currently.
Respiratory: Dyspnea on minimal exertion, progressive over recent weeks. No cough, hemoptysis, or chest pain.
Gastrointestinal: Anorexia, reduced oral intake. Mild intermittent nausea. No vomiting, diarrhea, melena, or hematochezia. No jaundice or right upper quadrant pain (though labs notable for rising bilirubin).
Musculoskeletal/Neurological: Diffuse bone pain, rated 5/10. No new focal neurological deficits. No limb weakness.
Genitourinary: Urinary output appears adequate; no gross hematuria. No new obstructive symptoms.
Hematologic: Ongoing fatigue and dyspnea consistent with known anemia. No spontaneous bleeding or bruising noted by patient.
Psychosocial: Patient and family aware of terminal trajectory. No acute suicidal ideation. Patient reports feeling at peace with situation but wishes to maximize comfort. Anxiety regarding pain control.

---

PAST MEDICAL AND ONCOLOGIC HISTORY (carried forward from prior notes)
- Metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC): Gleason 9 (5+4), initially mHSPC, progressed through ADT+abiraterone, enzalutamide, olaparib (BRCA2 germline), now progressing on carboplatin+cabazitaxel. Now with hepatic and pulmonary metastases and extensive osseous and nodal disease.
- Germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant (p.E1308*): confirmed at diagnosis, genetic counseling provided.
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus: on metformin (dose held/reduced given declining eGFR).
- Hypertension: on lisinopril 20 mg daily and amlodipine 10 mg daily.
- Chronic kidney disease, stage 3b: baseline creatinine 1.4 mg/dL at diagnosis; now 1.8 mg/dL (worsening with disease and prior AKI during cycle 1 hospitalization).
- Febrile neutropenia (cycle 1 carboplatin/cabazitaxel): hospitalized x5 days, resolved.
- Recurrent transfusion-dependent anemia: multiple pRBC transfusions since initiation of olaparib; most recently during cycle 1 hospitalization.
- Palliative radiation to right femoral neck/proximal femur: 30 Gy/10 fx, completed at age 70.3 years.
- Family history: brother with pancreatic cancer at 58, father with prostate cancer at 74.

ALLERGIES: No known drug allergies.

SOCIAL HISTORY: Retired steelworker. Married, lives with wife. Two adult children who are actively involved in his care. Former smoker (45 pack-years, quit at 62). Rare alcohol use. No illicit drug use. Advanced directive in place (DNR/DNI); wife designated as healthcare proxy (DPOA).

CURRENT MEDICATIONS:
1. Leuprolide acetate 22.5 mg IM every 3 months (ADT; continued)
2. Oxycodone extended-release 20 mg orally every 12 hours (initiated at prior visit for bone pain)
3. Oxycodone immediate-release 5 mg orally every 4 hours as needed for breakthrough pain
4. Ondansetron 8 mg orally every 8 hours as needed for nausea
5. Prochlorperazine 10 mg orally every 6 hours as needed for nausea (backup)
6. Zoledronic acid 4 mg IV every 12 weeks (bone-directed therapy; dose-adjusted for CKD)
7. Lisinopril 20 mg orally daily
8. Amlodipine 10 mg orally daily
9. Metformin 500 mg orally twice daily (dose reduced; held when Cr elevated; currently at borderline eGFR)
10. Ferrous sulfate 325 mg orally daily
11. Docusate sodium 100 mg orally twice daily (bowel regimen)
12. Senna 2 tablets orally at bedtime
Recently discontinued: Carboplatin (discontinued as of today), Cabazitaxel (discontinued as of today), Olaparib (discontinued at age 71 years), Enzalutamide (discontinued at age 70.2 years), Abiraterone/prednisone (discontinued at age 69 years).

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

General: Mr. Kowalski appears chronically ill and cachectic. He is seated in a wheelchair brought from the parking lot by family. He is alert, oriented x3, cooperative, and communicative, though visibly fatigued. He is in mild distress at rest, improved with repositioning.

Vital Signs:
- Blood pressure: 104/68 mmHg (hypotensive relative to baseline; prior readings 130-150 systolic range)
- Heart rate: 98 bpm (resting tachycardia)
- Respiratory rate: 18 breaths/min
- SpO2: 94% on room air (mildly reduced)
- Temperature: 37.1°C
- Weight: 68.1 kg (down from 82 kg at cancer diagnosis 4 years ago; 15 lbs lost over past 5 months; total loss ~30 lbs over disease course)
- Height: 5'10" (178 cm)
- BMI: 21.5 kg/m2 (low-normal, overtly cachectic appearance)

ECOG Performance Status: 3 (in bed or chair for more than 50% of waking hours; limited self-care; cannot carry out any work activity).

Skin/HEENT: Pallor of conjunctivae and mucous membranes consistent with anemia. No icterus. Temporal wasting. Dry mucous membranes. No oral lesions.

Neck: No palpable cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.

Cardiovascular: Tachycardic at rest, regular rhythm. S1/S2 without murmurs. No S3 or S4. Bilateral lower extremity pitting edema 1+ to the mid-shin, left slightly greater than right.

Respiratory: Mildly increased work of breathing. Breath sounds clear bilaterally on auscultation; no wheezing, rales, or egophony. Respiratory rate mildly elevated at rest.

Abdomen: Soft, mildly distended. Mild tenderness to deep palpation in right upper quadrant without rebound or guarding. Liver edge palpable approximately 3 cm below the right costal margin, firm in texture (hepatomegaly consistent with known hepatic metastases). No splenomegaly. No ascites appreciated on exam (no shifting dullness). Bowel sounds present.

Musculoskeletal: Diffuse tenderness with palpation along the lumbar spine and pelvis. No midline spinal tenderness. Range of motion of hips limited by pain, worse on left side. Right hip exam limited to observation (prior radiation field). No gross deformity. No calf tenderness.

Neurological: Alert and oriented. Cranial nerves grossly intact. Motor strength 4/5 symmetrically in bilateral lower extremities (limited by deconditioning and pain). Sensation grossly intact in all four extremities. No saddle anesthesia reported or suspected. No long tract signs. Reflexes not formally tested given clinical context.

Lymph nodes: No peripheral lymphadenopathy on exam. No axillary or inguinal nodes palpable (inguinal exam limited given discomfort).

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (from today's visit, drawn prior to clinic)

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 4.1 K/uL (recovered post-febrile neutropenia episode, within normal limits)
- ANC: 2.8 K/uL
- Hemoglobin: 7.1 g/dL (LOW; anemia grade 3 by CTCAE; down from 8.8 g/dL post-transfusion at start of carboplatin/cabazitaxel)
- Hematocrit: 21.4%
- MCV: 94 fL (normocytic)
- Platelets: 89,000/uL (LOW; mild thrombocytopenia; improved from nadir of 78,000)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 133 mEq/L (mildly low, hyponatremia likely multifactorial)
- Potassium: 4.2 mEq/L
- Chloride: 100 mEq/L
- CO2: 22 mEq/L
- BUN: 38 mg/dL (elevated)
- Creatinine: 1.8 mg/dL (eGFR calculated ~36 mL/min; stage 4 CKD range, progression from baseline)
- Glucose: 142 mg/dL (mildly elevated, consistent with diabetes and stress response)
- Total protein: 5.9 g/dL (low)
- Albumin: 2.7 g/dL (LOW; hypoalbuminemia reflecting malnutrition and systemic disease)
- Total bilirubin: 1.8 mg/dL (elevated; rising from 0.9 mg/dL 3 months ago; direct bilirubin 0.9 mg/dL)
- Direct bilirubin: 0.9 mg/dL
- AST: 68 U/L (elevated)
- ALT: 52 U/L (elevated)
- Alkaline phosphatase: 418 U/L (markedly elevated; up from 287 U/L at prior visit; reflects both osseous and hepatic disease)
- LDH: 689 U/L (markedly elevated; disease burden)
- Calcium: 9.8 mg/dL (normal)

Other:
- PSA: 18.6 ng/mL (rising; drawn at time of restaging imaging approximately 2-3 weeks ago; confirmed progressive trajectory)
- Testosterone: 18 ng/dL (castrate confirmed)
- PT/INR: 1.3 (mildly elevated, hepatic synthetic dysfunction)
- Magnesium: 1.8 mg/dL (low-normal)
- Phosphorus: 3.4 mg/dL (normal)

---

IMAGING RESULTS (reviewed from restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, read approximately 2-3 weeks ago)

Restaging CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis (most recent, reviewed today):
Liver: The two hypodense lesions in segments VI-VII of the right hepatic lobe, previously measuring 1.2 cm and 1.0 cm on the study prior to chemotherapy initiation, have grown to 1.4 cm and 1.3 cm respectively. A new 0.6 cm lesion has appeared in segment V. These findings are consistent with disease progression in the liver despite two cycles of carboplatin and cabazitaxel. The dominant right lobe lesions were biopsy-proven as metastatic adenocarcinoma of prostatic origin with neuroendocrine features.

Lungs: The previously indeterminate right lower lobe pulmonary nodule has increased to 9 mm (from 5 mm prior to chemotherapy initiation) and the second right lower lobe nodule has grown to 6 mm (from 4 mm). Both nodules now demonstrate interval growth on serial imaging, which in the clinical context of progressive mCRPC is concerning for pulmonary metastases. No pleural effusion on the most recent CT.

Pelvis/Retroperitoneum: Right obturator lymph node has enlarged to 2.4 cm (previously 1.6 cm at start of carboplatin/cabazitaxel; and 2.1 cm at time of enzalutamide progression). Para-aortic and aortocaval nodes appear stable.

Bones: Not adequately characterized on CT alone; bone scan performed at time of enzalutamide progression showed approximately 17 osseous lesions. Clinical symptoms and rising ALP/LDH are consistent with progressive or at minimum stable-to-active osseous disease.

---

ASSESSMENT

Mr. Kowalski is a 71-year-old male with end-stage mCRPC who has now progressed through five lines of systemic therapy (ADT+abiraterone, enzalutamide, olaparib, and carboplatin+cabazitaxel), as well as palliative radiation to the right femoral neck. He carries a germline BRCA2 p.E1308* mutation and has acquired AR amplification, AR L702H LBD mutation, and other resistance alterations as detected on his hepatic metastasis NGS. Pathology from his liver biopsy demonstrated features of early treatment-emergent neuroendocrine differentiation (focal synaptophysin positivity, Ki-67 ~60%). His disease has now spread to the liver and lungs, representing visceral crisis-level progression. His functional status has declined to ECOG 3, and laboratory data reveal progressive hepatic compromise (rising bilirubin, ALP, LDH, mild coagulopathy), significant hypoalbuminemia, and worsening renal function.

The current restaging imaging confirms progression through carboplatin and cabazitaxel. There are no further standard-of-care systemic therapy options that would be appropriate in the context of his ECOG 3 performance status, hepatic dysfunction (bilirubin 1.8 mg/dL, albumin 2.7 g/dL, INR 1.3), worsening renal function (eGFR ~36), transfusion-dependent anemia, and the overall trajectory of his disease. Specific considerations are as follows:

1. Lutetium-177 PSMA-617 (Pluvicto): Though PSMA expression was confirmed on prior imaging in this patient, his current hepatic functional impairment (hyperbilirubinemia, hypoalbuminemia), severe anemia (Hgb 7.1 g/dL), and ECOG 3 performance status represent significant relative contraindications. Lutetium-177 requires reasonably preserved bone marrow reserve, hepatic and renal function, and adequate performance status. He does not meet criteria for safe administration at this time. This option is not feasible.

2. Clinical trial: I reviewed available trials for which he might be eligible. Given his ECOG 3, visceral metastases with hepatic compromise, and rapidly deteriorating functional status, he does not meet eligibility criteria for any available phase I or II trials at our institution at this time. The TRITON3 platform and other BRCA-focused trials require higher performance status.

3. Re-challenge with prior agents or novel hormonal therapies: Given AR amplification and AR L702H mutation (known to confer resistance to abiraterone and possible enzalutamide cross-resistance), rechallenge with any AR-targeted therapy is unlikely to be beneficial and would carry significant toxicity risk in this patient.

4. Additional platinum or taxane chemotherapy: Given his prior toxicity with febrile neutropenia grade 4, grade 3 anemia, AKI, and now ECOG 3, further chemotherapy would be unsafe and potentially harmful.

Conclusion: Further cancer-directed systemic therapy is not appropriate or advisable at this time. The therapeutic decision is to discontinue carboplatin and cabazitaxel. Leuprolide (ADT) will be continued as it may provide marginal benefit in terms of testosterone-driven symptom control without significant additional toxicity. Goals of care have shifted entirely to comfort-focused management.

---

PLAN

1. Disease Status / Oncologic Management:
   - DISCONTINUE carboplatin and cabazitaxel effective today. No further active anti-cancer systemic therapy is planned.
   - CONTINUE leuprolide acetate (ADT) for testosterone suppression; next injection due in approximately 6 weeks and should be arranged through hospice or PCP as appropriate.
   - Zoledronic acid: Will discuss discontinuation with hospice team. Given declining renal function (eGFR 36) and transition to comfort care, the risk-benefit of continued bone-directed therapy should be reassessed. Defer to hospice team and patient/family preference.
   - Metformin: Given eGFR 36 and declining hepatic function, HOLD metformin to avoid lactic acidosis risk. Diabetes management can be simplified in the hospice setting; de-escalate glucose monitoring as appropriate.

2. Anemia:
   - Hemoglobin 7.1 g/dL; patient is symptomatic (dyspnea, fatigue). Will arrange packed red blood cell transfusion today (2 units) for symptomatic palliation. Patient has expressed preference to continue transfusions for comfort and quality of life.
   - Ongoing transfusion support (approximately monthly) can be coordinated through hospice or outpatient infusion per patient/family wishes. This decision will be revisited regularly in the hospice setting.
   - Discontinue ferrous sulfate given that anemia is not iron-deficiency mediated and this is now comfort-focused care.

3. Pain Management:
   - Current regimen of oxycodone ER 20 mg BID plus oxycodone IR 5 mg Q4H PRN is providing inadequate control (pain 5/10 at rest).
   - Transitioning to transdermal fentanyl patch: initiate fentanyl 25 mcg/hour patch every 72 hours. Continue oxycodone IR 5 mg Q4H PRN for breakthrough in the short term; hospice team to reassess and optimize.
   - Pain service and hospice team will take over primary pain management; verbal handoff provided today.
   - Bowel regimen: Continue senna and docusate. Add polyethylene glycol (MiraLax) PRN for opioid-related constipation, which is expected to worsen with fentanyl patch.

4. Dyspnea:
   - Mild dyspnea at rest/minimal exertion, SpO2 94% on room air. Multifactorial (anemia, pulmonary metastases, possible early lymphangitic disease).
   - Consider supplemental low-flow oxygen (2 L/min by nasal cannula) for symptomatic relief; home oxygen prescription provided today.
   - Low-dose oral morphine (2.5-5 mg every 4 hours PRN) can be used for dyspnea in addition to fentanyl patch, per hospice team's discretion.

5. Nausea:
   - Continue ondansetron 8 mg every 8 hours PRN and prochlorperazine 10 mg every 6 hours PRN for nausea/vomiting.
   - Consider dexamethasone 2-4 mg daily for appetite stimulation, nausea, and possible improvement in sense of well-being, if consistent with patient/family goals in hospice setting.

6. Nutrition:
   - Albumin 2.7 g/dL; significant weight loss and anorexia. In the context of hospice, aggressive nutritional supplementation and enteral/parenteral nutrition are not recommended and are not consistent with goals of care. Encourage small, frequent oral intake of preferred foods. Consider oral nutritional supplements (e.g., Ensure or equivalent) if tolerable.
   - Formal dietitian referral deferred; hospice team will provide support as appropriate.

7. Palliative Care / Hospice:
   - Palliative care team met with patient and family today (in-room consultation). An extensive goals-of-care discussion was held with Mr. Kowalski, his wife, and his adult daughter. The patient clearly articulated his understanding that his cancer is no longer responding to treatment and that his illness is terminal. He wishes to prioritize comfort, minimize hospitalizations, and spend remaining time at home with his family.
   - ENROLL in home hospice today. Hospice agency referral placed and confirmed. Hospice nurse will conduct initial home visit within 24 hours.
   - Advanced directive is in place: DNR/DNI. Wife is designated healthcare proxy (DPOA). These were reviewed and reconfirmed with patient today.
   - Discussed anticipated disease trajectory: progressive hepatic failure, increasing analgesic requirements, worsening fatigue, and ultimately decrease in consciousness. Family counseled on signs and symptoms of end-of-life and how hospice will support them.

8. Renal Function:
   - eGFR ~36; stage 4 CKD. Nephrology follow-up not indicated in this setting. Hydration and monitoring per hospice team.
   - Lisinopril: may continue at current dose for blood pressure management; reassess as clinical condition evolves (hypotension may develop with declining intake).

9. Hypertension:
   - BP today 104/68 mmHg -- lower than usual, likely reflecting poor PO intake, deconditioning, and possibly early distributive physiology from hepatic disease.
   - Reduce lisinopril to 10 mg daily. Consider holding amlodipine. Hospice team to monitor and titrate antihypertensives as appropriate; de-escalation likely.

10. Follow-up:
    - No further oncology follow-up scheduled at this time given transition to hospice. Patient and family may contact us directly if questions arise.
    - Hospice team and PCP will assume primary care coordination.
    - Family provided with contact information for after-hours oncology triage line should urgent questions arise prior to hospice establishment.

Time spent with patient and family today: approximately 75 minutes, the majority of which was counseling and care coordination.

I want to express to Mr. Kowalski and his family how much it has been our honor to care for him. He has fought this illness with remarkable courage and dignity, and his priorities throughout have been clear and consistently articulated. We are committed to supporting him through this next phase of his journey.

[Electronically signed by Attending Genitourinary Oncologist]
[Dictated and finalized same day]
